---
title: "Inverting basal shear stress"
---

The plastic solve turns a margin, a bed, and a basal-shear-stress field $\tau_b$ into an ice
sheet. $\tau_b$ carries essentially all of the reconstruction's freedom, so the practical
question is the **inverse** one: what $\tau_b$ makes the reconstruction match data? This page
covers how that inverse problem is posed, the two parameterizations of $\tau_b$ the examples
provide (and how to switch between them), and how this relates to — and automates — the
hand-tuned shear-stress domains of ICESHEET (Gowan et al. 2016, 2021).

The gradient machinery itself (implicit differentiation of the converged eikonal) lives in
[Algorithms §7](algorithms.qmd#sec-ad); here we use it.

## The inverse problem

Pick an objective on the reconstruction and minimise it over $\tau_b$. For present-day ice
the natural target is the observed surface, through `surface_misfit`:

$$
\min_{\tau_b}\; \big\langle\, (z_s(\tau_b) - z_s^{\text{obs}})^2 \,\big\rangle_{\text{mask}},
\qquad z_s(\tau_b) = z_b + H(\tau_b).
$$

`differentiable_thickness` gives $\partial H/\partial\tau_b$ by reverse-mode AD, so any
gradient optimiser (the examples use Adam) drives the misfit down. The grounded `mask` is the
prescribed margin — an ICESHEET input, **not** something inverted — so the recovered ice
*extent* always equals the mask; only the *geometry* inside it is fit.

The number of free parameters in $\tau_b$ should match what the target data can resolve. A
dense present-day surface constrains a dense field; a sparse paleo target (relative sea
level) constrains only a handful of regional values. The two parameterizations below sit at
those two ends.

## Two parameterizations

The example
[`examples/invert_taub_greenland.jl`](https://github.com/fesmc/PlasticIceSheet.jl/blob/main/examples/invert_taub_greenland.jl)
implements both and switches between them with a single environment variable, `METHOD`:

```bash
julia --project=examples examples/invert_taub_greenland.jl                  # METHOD=tiled (default)
METHOD=percell julia --project=examples examples/invert_taub_greenland.jl   # point-by-point
```

Everything downstream — the objective, the Adam loop, the outputs — is shared; only the map
from optimisation parameters to the $\tau_b$ field differs.

### Point-by-point (`METHOD=percell`)

A full per-cell $\tau_b$ field, regularized to a length scale `REG_KM` by a **Gaussian-blur
reparameterization**: optimise a raw field $\varphi$ and set

$$
\tau_b = \operatorname{clamp}\big(K_x\,\varphi\,K_y\big),
$$

with $K_x, K_y$ separable 1-D Gaussian smoothers ($\sigma = \texttt{REG\_KM}/\Delta x$ cells).
Being linear, the blur differentiates cleanly under Zygote, and it *guarantees* $\tau_b$
varies only on scales $\gtrsim$ `REG_KM` regardless of the data fit. Many effective degrees of
freedom; fits a dense observed surface closely (on Greenland GRL-32KM, ~85 m rmse).

### Tiled (`METHOD=tiled`, default)

A low-dimensional, **paleo-transferable** parameterization — the automatic version of
ICESHEET's hand-tuned shear-stress domains. Cells are grouped into tiles defined **only from
data available for an unknown ice sheet**: bedrock roughness (windowed standard deviation of
$z_b$, binned into `N_ROUGH` quantile classes) crossed with a sediment mask (`z_sed`,
split soft/hard at `SED_THRESH`). The ice thickness and ice-derived drainage basins are never
used to draw the tiles. Each tile $d$ carries one scalar $\theta_d$:

$$
\tau_b(\mathbf x) = \operatorname{clamp}\Big(\textstyle\sum_d \theta_d\, \mathbb 1[\,\mathbf x \in d\,]\Big),
$$

embedded via a one-hot selector matrix (linear, hence Zygote-friendly). The default is
`N_ROUGH = 4` × 2 sediment classes = **8 tiles**. The observed surface enters *only* as the
calibration target — exactly the modern-surface calibration Gowan used for Greenland and
Antarctica, and the slot a relative-sea-level objective would occupy for a paleo
reconstruction.

## Relation to ICESHEET / Gowan et al.

ICESHEET (Gowan et al. 2016) is the same perfectly-plastic, equilibrium forward model. In
PaleoMIST (Gowan et al. 2021) $\tau_b$ was chosen by:

- **Target.** Relative sea level through a GIA model for the paleo ice sheets (adjust
  $\tau_b$ to maximise LGM volume, reduce it through deglaciation to fit postglacial
  sea-level indicators), plus geological margins; and the **modern surface** for Greenland and
  Antarctica.
- **Domains.** Defined by topographic variability (mountainous vs flat) refined by surficial
  geology (continuous sediment → low $\tau_b$; hard/discontinuous → high), with boundaries on
  fjords, mountain ranges, and the flat interior.
- **Tuning.** Manual and iterative — repeated by hand until the fit was acceptable.

The `tiled` method reproduces this logic but **automates both halves**: the domains come from
bed roughness + sediment programmatically, and the per-domain values come from gradient
descent rather than hand-tuning. On Greenland it recovers exactly Gowan's premise — soft-
sediment tiles collapse to 5–32 kPa, hard-bed tiles sit at 60–130 kPa.

## How many tiles? A resolution test

The one judgement ICESHEET left to intuition — how many domains — can be made quantitative.
[`examples/invert_taub_sweep_greenland.jl`](https://github.com/fesmc/PlasticIceSheet.jl/blob/main/examples/invert_taub_sweep_greenland.jl)
sweeps the tile count and plots surface-misfit rmse against the number of free parameters; the
**knee** of that curve is the data-supported resolution. On Greenland GRL-32KM:

- **Sediment geology dominates, not roughness count.** A single hard/soft split (2 tiles)
  drops rmse from 514 → 356 m — more than eight roughness-only tiles achieve (446 m).
- **The knee is early — ~6–8 tiles.** Beyond it the curve flattens (6 tiles → 333 m, 15
  tiles → 327 m): extra parameters do not earn their keep.
- **An irreducible floor.** Even the best tiling (~327 m) sits well above the per-cell fit
  (~85 m), so a substantial part of the surface signal reflects $\tau_b$ variability that
  static bed roughness and sediment cannot explain (basal thermal/hydrological state).

## Conditioning note: `:flat` vs `:full`

The inversions use `mode = :flat`. The `:full` adjoint forms a dense Jacobian over the whole
grid, whose direct linear solve becomes singular/ill-posed on large grids; `:flat` gives a
well-conditioned eikonal adjoint and ties $\tau_b$ directly to surface slope, which is the
more natural object when fitting a surface. For finer grids the per-cell gradient cost is
dominated by that dense solve — see the `differentiable_thickness` docstring for the
iterative-solver option.

## Toward a paleo (RSL) reconstruction

The `tiled` parameterization is the bridge to a genuine paleo reconstruction: keep the
bed-derived tiles, and replace the observed-surface objective with relative sea level routed
through a GIA model. Because the tiles need no ice-sheet knowledge to define, the same setup
transfers to an ice sheet whose geometry is unknown — which is exactly the regime ICESHEET
was built for, and where reducing the parameter count is not optional but required.
